evaluation_inputs.system_description: WIN-T is the Army's tactical telecommunications system designed to provide a secure, high-speed, high-capacity, and high-mobility backbone communications network. It enables mobile communications using the Internet Protocol (IP) to provide integrated voice, data, and video. It connects the Global Information Grid (GIG) to the Future Force, extending from the Maneuver Battalion to the Theater Rear boundary.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The documentation was recently consolidated following the merger of two competing contractor teams. As a result, the initial architecture documentation contained inconsistencies, was excessively complex, and diagrams often had mixed heritage, leading to confusion and potential errors.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How will the system handle the self-organization of nodes when requirements for the algorithms are not fully defined?
What are the specific interface definitions for the Service-Oriented Architecture given the reliance on emerging standards?
How does the architecture accommodate the transition from radio firmware to high-level network operations without explicit performance models?

hardware_view_description: The system must support mobile nodes (vehicles) communicating via Line-of-Sight radios and satellite links.
The topology includes tiered nodes ranging from Maneuver Battalions to Theater Rear boundaries, requiring 'on-the-move' connectivity.
runtime_constraints: Network bandwidth is severely limited during peak operational traffic, requiring strict QoS throttling.
The system must support 'on-the-move' throughput while traveling at speeds up to 25-45 mph.

questions: How does the Enterprise Application Integration Platform handle message latency across low-bandwidth satellite links?
How is loose coupling maintained between the NetOps management software and the radio transmission subsystems?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: A collection of vehicles collects in a 'parking lot' during operations. The networks can self-organize and nodes configure themselves within 5 minutes.
A COTS product is becoming increasingly incompatible with other COTS products being used. The COTS product is replaced.
A unit is overrun during operations. The system detects the unauthorized users and generates a security alert; an authorized person can 'zeroize' the system and shut it down remotely.
A WIN-T gateway node has been corrupted due to a network intrusion. Intrusion and corruption is detected and another node takes over duties.

business_goals: Provide a single integrating communications network to function as the Army's tactical portion of the GIG
Link FCS with higher Army echelons and the GIG
Provide a reliable and secure network with high speed, high capacity, and high quality of service (QoS)
Support mobile communications and replace legacy systems (MSE, TRI-TAC, ISYSCON, Trojan Spirit)
Deploy a user-friendly human machine interface (HMI) for the WIN-T operator
Minimize the amount of network management the WIN-T operator must perform (automate planning)
Operable and maintainable within budget and schedule
Transparent to combat users and operators
technical_constraints: Long life cycle (fielding from 2008 through 2020, expected lifetime 20+ years)
Interoperable with Army Enterprise network, FCS, Joint networks, and Coalition network
Upgradeable (early spirals, block upgrades, COTS upgrades, security upgrades)

architectural_approaches.description: The system uses SOA with an Enterprise Application Integration Platform to enable loose coupling between NetOps management software and radio transmission subsystems, supporting interoperability and modularity.

addressed_attributes.rationale: SOA enables integration with Army Enterprise network, FCS, Joint networks, and Coalition networks through standard service interfaces.
Loose coupling allows independent evolution of network management and radio subsystems.
Service-based architecture supports COTS upgrades and block upgrades over the long lifecycle.

architectural_approaches.description: An Enterprise Application Integration Platform using message bus architecture to handle communication across distributed mobile nodes with varying bandwidth constraints.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Message bus supports tiered node topology from Maneuver Battalions to Theater Rear boundaries.
Enables management of message latency across low-bandwidth satellite links with QoS throttling.

architectural_approaches.description: Nodes can autonomously configure and organize themselves when vehicles collect in operational areas, supporting dynamic topology changes.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Automatic network self-organization handles dynamic battlefield conditions without manual intervention.
Supports on-the-move connectivity at speeds up to 25-45 mph with automatic reconfiguration.
Minimizes network management burden on WIN-T operators through automation.

architectural_approaches.description: Multiple nodes can assume duties of corrupted or compromised nodes, ensuring continuous operation even under attack or failure conditions.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Achieves 0.98 reliability at halt and 0.90 mobile through redundant node architecture.
System continues operating when individual nodes are corrupted or fail.
Supports detection of unauthorized users and remote zeroization in overrun scenarios.

architectural_approaches.description: Multi-layered information assurance approach including intrusion detection, corruption detection, and remote shutdown capabilities.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Protects against 95% of attacks through comprehensive security measures.
Provides secure network with detection and response capabilities for intrusions and compromises.

children.refinement: Mobile Throughput: On-the-move connectivity at 25-45 mph
children.scenario_description: Vehicles moving at operational speeds (25-45 mph) maintain network connectivity with acceptable throughput for tactical communications.

children.refinement: Message Latency: QoS throttling across low-bandwidth satellite links
children.scenario_description: Messages traverse tiered topology from Maneuver Battalion to Theater Rear with managed latency despite bandwidth constraints on satellite links.

children.refinement: Failover capability: 0.98 reliability at halt, 0.90 mobile

children.refinement: Cross-network integration: Army Enterprise, FCS, Joint, Coalition networks

children.scenario_description: RS-18: A unit is overrun during operations. The system detects the unauthorized users and generates a security alert; an authorized person can 'zeroize' the system and shut it down remotely.
System detects 95% of network intrusion attempts and triggers appropriate security responses to maintain information assurance.
RS-69: A collection of vehicles collects in a 'parking lot' during operations. The networks can self-organize and nodes configure themselves within 5 minutes.

children.scenario_description: RS-49: A COTS product is becoming increasingly incompatible with other COTS products being used. The COTS product is replaced.

children.scenario_description: WIN-T operators manage the network with minimal manual intervention due to automated self-organization and configuration capabilities.

scenarios.scenario_text: Vehicles moving at operational speeds (25-45 mph) maintain network connectivity with acceptable throughput for tactical communications.
Messages traverse tiered topology from Maneuver Battalion to Theater Rear with managed latency despite bandwidth constraints on satellite links.
A WIN-T gateway node has been corrupted due to a network intrusion. Intrusion and corruption is detected and another node takes over duties.
WIN-T system integrates with Army Enterprise network and FCS under normal operations, exchanging tactical data through standard service interfaces.
A unit is overrun during operations. The system detects the unauthorized users and generates a security alert; an authorized person can 'zeroize' the system and shut it down remotely.
System detects 95% of network intrusion attempts and triggers appropriate security responses to maintain information assurance.
A collection of vehicles collects in a 'parking lot' during operations. The networks can self-organize and nodes configure themselves within 5 minutes.
A COTS product is becoming increasingly incompatible with other COTS products being used. The COTS product is replaced.
System scales across tiered node topology supporting hundreds of mobile nodes from forward Maneuver Battalions through Theater Rear boundaries.
WIN-T operators manage the network with minimal manual intervention due to automated self-organization and configuration capabilities.

scenario_reference.text: Vehicles moving at operational speeds (25-45 mph) maintain network connectivity with acceptable throughput for tactical communications.

analysis_records.environment: Mobile operations with vehicles moving at 25-45 mph in tactical battlefield conditions
analysis_records.stimulus: Vehicles moving at operational speeds require continuous network connectivity
analysis_records.response_measure: Maintain acceptable throughput for tactical communications while mobile at 25-45 mph
architectural_decisions: Self-organizing network architecture enables automatic topology reconfiguration as nodes move
Message bus pattern with QoS management handles variable connectivity
Redundant node architecture maintains connectivity during handoffs
analysis_records.reasoning: The self-organizing network approach allows nodes to dynamically reconfigure as vehicles move, maintaining connectivity through automatic handoffs. The message bus with QoS throttling manages bandwidth constraints during mobile operations. Redundancy ensures continuous service during topology changes.

risks.description: Network reconfiguration latency during high-speed movement may cause temporary connectivity gaps
risks.rationale: At 45 mph, vehicles cover significant distance during the 5-minute self-organization window, potentially causing service interruptions during rapid topology changes

risks.description: Insufficient bandwidth on satellite links during mobile operations may degrade throughput below acceptable levels
risks.rationale: Mobile nodes rely on satellite links with inherent bandwidth constraints; simultaneous movement of multiple nodes could saturate available bandwidth

non_risks.description: Message loss during handoffs between nodes
non_risks.rationale: Message bus architecture with store-and-forward capabilities and redundant nodes ensures message persistence during topology transitions

scenario_reference.text: Messages traverse tiered topology from Maneuver Battalion to Theater Rear with managed latency despite bandwidth constraints on satellite links.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with tiered network topology spanning Maneuver Battalion to Theater Rear
analysis_records.stimulus: Messages need to traverse multiple network tiers across low-bandwidth satellite links
analysis_records.response_measure: Latency managed through QoS throttling to meet tactical communication requirements
architectural_decisions: Message bus pattern with QoS throttling prioritizes critical messages
Tiered topology with hierarchical message routing
Enterprise Application Integration Platform manages message flow across tiers
analysis_records.reasoning: The message bus architecture enables priority-based message routing across bandwidth-constrained links. QoS throttling ensures critical tactical messages receive priority over lower-priority traffic. The tiered topology minimizes unnecessary message propagation.

risks.description: Message queue overflow at tier boundaries during high-traffic scenarios
risks.rationale: Satellite link bandwidth constraints combined with high message volume could cause queue buildup at tier gateways, leading to increased latency or message drops

non_risks.rationale: SOA with Enterprise Application Integration Platform provides standardized service interfaces and routing logic, ensuring reliable message delivery across network tiers

sensitivity_points.parameter: Message queue size at tier gateways

conflicting_attributes: Usability (negative: lower-priority messages may be significantly delayed)

scenario_reference.text: A WIN-T gateway node has been corrupted due to a network intrusion. Intrusion and corruption is detected and another node takes over duties.

analysis_records.environment: Operational network under attack with node corruption
analysis_records.stimulus: Gateway node becomes corrupted due to network intrusion
analysis_records.response_measure: Achieve 0.98 reliability at halt and 0.90 mobile through automatic failover
architectural_decisions: Redundancy and failover architecture with multiple nodes capable of assuming gateway duties
Layered security architecture with intrusion and corruption detection
Self-organizing network enables automatic reconfiguration after node failure
analysis_records.reasoning: The redundant node architecture ensures that when a gateway node is corrupted, other nodes can immediately assume its duties. The layered security architecture detects intrusions and corruption, triggering failover mechanisms. Self-organization capabilities enable automatic network reconfiguration without manual intervention.

risks.description: Failover transition time may cause temporary service disruption
risks.rationale: The time required to detect corruption, trigger failover, and reconfigure the network could result in brief connectivity loss, particularly impacting mobile reliability target of 0.90

risks.description: Cascading failures if multiple nodes are simultaneously compromised
risks.rationale: Sophisticated coordinated attacks targeting multiple redundant nodes could overwhelm failover capabilities and degrade reliability below target thresholds

non_risks.description: Complete network failure from single node corruption
non_risks.rationale: Redundant architecture ensures multiple nodes can perform gateway functions, preventing single points of failure

conflicting_attributes: Network Reliability (negative: false positives trigger unnecessary failovers)

scenario_reference.text: WIN-T system integrates with Army Enterprise network and FCS under normal operations, exchanging tactical data through standard service interfaces.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations requiring integration with multiple external networks
analysis_records.stimulus: Need to exchange tactical data with Army Enterprise network, FCS, Joint networks, and Coalition networks
analysis_records.response_measure: Seamless data exchange through standard service interfaces
architectural_decisions: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) with standardized service interfaces
Enterprise Application Integration Platform for cross-network communication
Loose coupling between WIN-T and external network systems
analysis_records.reasoning: SOA provides standardized service interfaces that enable interoperability across heterogeneous networks. The Enterprise Application Integration Platform acts as a mediation layer, translating between different network protocols and data formats. Loose coupling ensures WIN-T can evolve independently while maintaining integration.

risks.description: Service interface version incompatibilities with external networks
risks.rationale: Different networks may evolve their service interfaces at different rates, potentially causing integration failures if version management is not properly handled

non_risks.description: Inability to integrate with new network types
non_risks.rationale: SOA architecture with Enterprise Application Integration Platform provides extensible framework for adding new network integrations through service interface definitions

conflicting_attributes: Interoperability (positive: higher abstraction enables broader compatibility)

scenario_reference.text: A unit is overrun during operations. The system detects the unauthorized users and generates a security alert; an authorized person can 'zeroize' the system and shut it down remotely.

analysis_records.environment: Hostile conditions with unit being overrun by enemy forces
analysis_records.stimulus: Unauthorized users attempt to access overrun system
analysis_records.response_measure: Detect unauthorized access, generate alert, and enable remote zeroization
architectural_decisions: Layered security architecture with unauthorized user detection
Remote management capabilities through SOA service interfaces
Secure authentication and authorization mechanisms
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered security architecture continuously monitors for unauthorized access attempts. When detected, the system generates alerts through the SOA-based management interface. Remote zeroization capability, implemented as a secure service, allows authorized personnel to destroy sensitive data and shut down the system from a safe location.

risks.description: Communication link to overrun unit may be compromised preventing remote zeroization
risks.rationale: If enemy forces control the physical location, they may be able to jam or intercept the remote zeroization command before it executes

risks.description: Delayed detection of unauthorized access allows data exfiltration
risks.rationale: Time gap between unit overrun and unauthorized user detection could allow adversaries to extract sensitive tactical information

non_risks.description: Unauthorized users gaining system access after zeroization
non_risks.rationale: Zeroization process destroys cryptographic keys and sensitive data, rendering the system unusable even if physically captured

conflicting_attributes: Security (positive: automatic zeroization prevents data loss)
Usability (negative: false triggers could disable operational systems)

scenario_reference.text: System detects 95% of network intrusion attempts and triggers appropriate security responses to maintain information assurance.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with ongoing intrusion attempts
analysis_records.stimulus: Network intrusion attempts from various threat vectors
analysis_records.response_measure: Detect 95% of attacks and trigger appropriate security responses
architectural_decisions: Layered security architecture with intrusion detection systems
Automated security response mechanisms
Multi-level information assurance controls
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered security architecture implements multiple detection mechanisms to achieve 95% attack detection rate. Automated response systems trigger countermeasures such as isolating compromised nodes, alerting operators, and initiating failover procedures. Multiple security layers provide defense-in-depth.

risks.description: 5% undetected attacks could compromise critical nodes
risks.rationale: The 95% detection rate means 1 in 20 attacks may go undetected, potentially allowing sophisticated adversaries to establish persistent access

risks.description: False positive intrusion detections may trigger unnecessary security responses
risks.rationale: Overly sensitive detection mechanisms could misidentify legitimate traffic as attacks, causing operational disruptions through false alarms and automated responses

non_risks.description: Undetected intrusions spreading across entire network
non_risks.rationale: Layered security with node isolation capabilities prevents lateral movement, containing intrusions even if initial detection fails

conflicting_attributes: Information Assurance (positive: deeper inspection catches more attacks)

scenario_reference.text: A collection of vehicles collects in a 'parking lot' during operations. The networks can self-organize and nodes configure themselves within 5 minutes.

analysis_records.environment: Operational pause with multiple vehicles collecting in assembly area
analysis_records.stimulus: Collection of vehicles in parking lot configuration requires network establishment
analysis_records.response_measure: Network self-organizes and nodes configure within 5 minutes
architectural_decisions: Self-organizing network architecture with autonomous configuration
Distributed coordination algorithms for node discovery and topology formation
Automated network management reducing operator burden
analysis_records.reasoning: The self-organizing network architecture enables nodes to automatically discover neighbors, negotiate roles, and establish network topology without manual configuration. Distributed algorithms coordinate the self-organization process to complete within the 5-minute requirement. This minimizes operator workload and enables rapid network establishment.

risks.description: Self-organization may exceed 5-minute target in large vehicle collections
risks.rationale: The complexity of distributed coordination increases with the number of nodes; large parking lot scenarios with many vehicles may require more time for convergence

risks.description: Conflicting node role assignments during self-organization
risks.rationale: Distributed algorithms may encounter race conditions or split-brain scenarios where multiple nodes attempt to assume the same role, requiring conflict resolution that extends configuration time

non_risks.description: Manual reconfiguration required after vehicle movement
non_risks.rationale: Self-organizing capability automatically adapts to topology changes, eliminating need for manual intervention when vehicles reposition

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of nodes in parking lot

conflicting_attributes: Network Reliability (negative: automated decisions may be suboptimal)

scenario_reference.text: A COTS product is becoming increasingly incompatible with other COTS products being used. The COTS product is replaced.

analysis_records.environment: Long lifecycle operations with evolving COTS product landscape
analysis_records.stimulus: COTS product incompatibility with other system components
analysis_records.response_measure: COTS product can be replaced without major system disruption
architectural_decisions: Service-Oriented Architecture with loose coupling between components
Standardized interfaces isolating COTS products from core system
Enterprise Application Integration Platform mediating COTS interactions
analysis_records.reasoning: SOA with loose coupling isolates COTS products behind service interfaces, allowing replacement without affecting other components. The Enterprise Application Integration Platform provides an abstraction layer that shields the system from COTS-specific implementation details. This enables COTS evolution and replacement over the long system lifecycle.

risks.description: COTS replacement may require extensive integration testing and validation
risks.rationale: Even with loose coupling, replacing COTS products requires verification that new components properly implement service interfaces and maintain system-level quality attributes

risks.description: Cascading incompatibilities when multiple COTS products evolve simultaneously
risks.rationale: If several COTS vendors update their products concurrently, managing multiple replacements and ensuring compatibility across the integration platform becomes complex

non_risks.description: Complete system redesign required for COTS replacement
non_risks.rationale: SOA architecture with standardized service interfaces enables component replacement without affecting core system architecture

sensitivity_points.parameter: COTS product coupling to system internals

conflicting_attributes: Modularity (positive: higher abstraction enables easier replacement)
Performance (negative: abstraction layers add overhead and latency)
